content:
Favour Iruoghene Agbaike

3 min read
Just now

--

I’m currently in Cohort 3 of the DevOps Micro Internship with Agentic AI, a hands-on program where we learn real DevOps skills by actually building things, not just watching videos. Week 2 was all about agentic AI tools inside Claude Code, teaching an AI assistant to understand a project through a file called CLAUDE.md, building reusable “skills” that turn long instructions into single commands, creating specialized subagents for tasks like security audits, connecting Claude to live external data through something called MCP, setting up safety hooks that block dangerous actions before they happen, and finally, teaching Claude to remember things across sessions using its memory system. It sounds like a lot, and honestly, it was, but I want to tell you not just what I learned technically, but what this week actually felt like to live through.
This has been an intense week. I work twelve hour shifts, and balancing that with this course has not been easy at all. I tried to plan thirty minutes to an hour each day for assignments, but some days that plan didn’t hold, and I ended up catching up on two or three days’ worth of work in one sitting on the days I was actually free. It hasn’t been smooth, but it has been honest, and I finished, which matters more to me than doing it perfectly.
1. Biggest technical insight I got this week
The biggest thing that clicked for me was understanding CLAUDE.md and realizing it isn’t documentation for people, it’s an instruction manual for the AI itself. I saw it directly, before I had that file, Claude gave generic answers about my project. The moment I wrote clear rules into it, Claude’s behavior actually changed, mentioning the exact deployment setup I described, and even refusing a request that broke one of my stated rules. Watching that shift happen in real time was the moment this all stopped feeling abstract.
2. Biggest insight I got about myself this week
I learned that I actually understand things best by breaking them and having to fix them myself, not by reading instructions passively. Outside of the assignments, I even built a shared inventory tracker for my care client’s home supplies this week, and I used what I’ve been learning about agentic AI to help me debug things that weren’t working. That moment reminded me how much this course is already spilling into real, useful work, not staying stuck inside a classroom.
3. My biggest weakness or loop I noticed
Procrastination. I know it too well, and this week reminded me why it never actually helps. It just piles things up, and then you either rush through them without truly understanding what you did, or you don’t finish at all. I saw this play out directly in my own week, and it pushed me to be more disciplined.
4. One system I will implement from this week
I will set a fixed daily time, right after my shift ends, even if it’s just thirty minutes, to touch the course in some way, rather than waiting for a “free day” to catch up all at once. Doing this consistently, even in small amounts, will help me avoid the rushed, piled up feeling I had this week.
5. What I learned about Agentic AI and DevOps
I learned that agentic AI is about structure and boundaries, not just conversation. Skills turn repeated instructions into single reliable commands, subagents let specialized work happen safely, hooks stop dangerous actions before they occur, and memory lets an AI carry context the way a real team member would. It doesn’t replace human judgment, it supports it.
6. My Week 2 highlight
Watching my /scaffold-terraform skill generate a full AWS infrastructure setup from one command, and then watching my security-auditor subagent calmly analyze that same code and report real findings by severity, felt genuinely like engineering, not just following steps. And doing all of that while also carrying a full work week reminded me that discipline, not multitasking, is what actually gets things done. One step at a time, fully understood, beats rushing through everything at once.
